Statements of Economic Interest
322-6444

March 9, 1983


George D. Lindberg
City Attorney
276 Fourth Avenue
Chula Vista, CA 92010

                                    Re: Your Opinion to Frank A.
                                        Scott, Our No. A-83-059

Dear Mr. Lindberg:

     On March 4, 1983, courtesy of James Hamilton, San Diego
District Attorney's Office, I received a copy of your opinion
memorandum dated February 17, 1983, to Frank A. Scott,
Councilmember. Although the memorandum (at page 7) reflects
that you were sending a copy to me for comment, none was ever
received.

     I have had two telephone conversations with you in the last
month. The first occurred on February 9th. You called to
inquire about the applicability of our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18740 to two councilmembers' circumstances. This
regulation permits, in certain limited circumstances, the
non-disclosure of privileged information otherwise required to
be reported on an official's Statement of Economic Interests.
We specifically discussed its applicability to two situations:
(1) someone who loans money to individuals, but who is not a
bank; and (2) someone who is a stockbroker. I advised you that
the application of Section 18740 is limited to legally
recognized privileges and, even then, would not apply in all
cases. I cited you to the case of Hays v. Wood, in which an
attorney asserted the attorney-client privilege, this privilege
was rejected as a basis for non-disclosure by the California
Supreme Court. Our first conversation did not involve any
discussion of disqualification.

     The second phone conversation occurred on February 15th. In
that conversation you asked me whether a councilmember who is a
broker working for Merrill Lynch has to disclose names of
clients who have paid him commissions. We discussed the
Commission's past advice in this regard and I advised you that

George Lindberg
March 9, 1983
Page 2


the broker would have to disclose at the $10,000 and above
level, but disqualification would be required at the $250 and
above level. I mentioned the Bennetti letter, (A-2123) and
you requested a copy of it, which I sent to you. You stated
your opinion that the councilmember's disqualification is not
required if the amount of his stockbroker commissions is not
affected by the decision. I explained to you that it is the
effect on the source of income which is determinative and cited
you to the case of Witt v. Morrow in addition to the plain
language of Government Code Section 87103.

You then advised me that you would probably be seeking the
Commission's advice as well as the Attorney General's because a
councilmember may have acted on a matter when he had a conflict
of interest. You were concerned about the contract that had
been voted on by this member. I explained that the contract
could not be challenged unless the vote or participation of the
councilmember with the conflict had been determinative.
(Government Code Section 91003(b).) You advised that the vote
on the matter had been 5-0. You wondered if the contract should
be rescinded by the council and voted on again. I explained
that this could be done if the councilmember wanted to do it for
political reasons, but that it would have no real effect since
the contract would presumably be approved again 4-0 and the
conflict, if any, had already occurred.

Upon reading your memorandum, I find that you apparently
misunderstood my advice on the recission and have advised your
clients that Mr. Scott's vote could be expunged. This is not
what I stated to you.

To recapitulate our advice, let me state the following:

1. A councilmember who is a stockbroker working at a firm
like Merrill Lynch will have to disclose those clients whose
transactions result in commissions to him of $10,000 or more
during the reporting period.

This is not based upon the councilmember's owning 10% or
more of Merrill Lynch, but is based upon his being in business
as a broker working through Merrill Lynch.

2. Disqualification by such a councilmember will be
required as to any decision which may foreseeably have a
material financial effect on a client who has generated $250 or
more in commissions for the member during the preceding 12-month
period.

George Lindberg
March 9, 1983
Page 3


     3.  Past violations of the disqualification provisions of
the Political Reform Act cannot be corrected by being expunged
from the record.

     This letter is in no way intended to imply any conclusion as
to whether or not Mr. Scott has violated the disqualification
provisions of the Act by voting on the contract.  The local
civil prosecutor which, in this instance, is the San Diego
County District Attorney, has the primary responsibility under
the Act of investigating conflicts of interest by local elected
officials.  Government Code Section 91001.  Mr. James Hamilton
of that office is aware of the situation; you should contact him
to find out what action, if any, his office plans to take in
this matter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

     San Diego County District Attorney's Office

CONFIDENTIAL

DATE:    February 17, 1983

TO:      Frank A. Scott, Councilman

FROM:    George D. Lindberg, City Attorney

SUBJECT: Request for Opinion on Possible Conflict of Interest

Pursuant to the provisions of Section 2.20.060 of the Chula Vista
Municipal Code, Councilman Frank Scott has requested a written
opinion from the City Attorney because of the uncertainty of the
application of the nature of the prohibitions as contained in the
conflicts of interest sections commencing with Section 87100 of
the Government Code, a part of the Political Reform Act.

The questions raised may be stated as follows:

1.   Would a conflict of interest arise under Section 87100 based
     upon the financial interests provisions of Section 87103 if
     the councilmember votes on an exclusive franchise for the
     Chula Vista Sanitary Service when the principal owner of said
     franchisee is a client of the councilman who is a stockbroker
     or account executive with whom said principal owner maintains
     an account and the councilman handles the buying and selling
     of his stocks and other investments receiving commissions in
     excess of $250 from said account?

2.   What are the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section
     87207 of the Government Code for a stockbroker or account
     executive whose total income is now based solely upon
     commissions received upon the sale or purchase of stocks
     which he handles for his clients?

The conclusions are:

1.   A conflict of interest does arise under Section 87100 based
     upon the financial interests provisions of Section 87103 if
     the councilmember votes on an exclusive franchise for the
     Chula Vista Sanitary Service when the principal owner of said
     franchisee is a client of the councilman who is a stockbroker
     or account executive with whom said principal owner maintains
     an account and the councilman handles the buying and selling
     of his stocks and other investments receiving commissions in
     excess of $250 from said account.

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Two


2.  A broker must file in accordance with the requirements of
Section 87207, subsection B(2), the name of every person who
paid fees to the business entity if the filer has some
investment interest in the business entity and his pro rata
share of fees from such person was equal to or greater than
ten thousand dollars ($10,000).

ANALYSIS:

On Friday, February 11, 1983, the City Attorney was requested by
Councilman Frank Scott to provide a written opinion concerning the
above questions principally the first of which was raised by Mr.
Clifford Roland, a member of the Board of Ethics of the City of
Chula Vista.  In addition to a review of the conflicts of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act, certain opinions of the
Attorney General and Appellate Court cases cited herein, the City
Attorney contacted other city attorneys in this area who have
presented papers to the League of California Cities concerning the
matter of conflict of interest.  As a result of this analysis and
basing the opinion primarily upon the fact that the decision
regarding the award of the franchise to Chula Vista Sanitary
Sanitary Service did not directly or materially affect the
financial interests of the councilman acting as a broker, the City
Attorney in a meeting with Mr. Roland and Councilman Scott
expressed the opinion that there was no conflict of interest.

He further indicated at that time that this viewpoint was shared
by those persons with whom he had discussed the matter and that he
had received no clear reading of the situation from Mr. Robert E.
Leidigh, Counsel for the State of California Fair Political
Practices Commission with whom he also discussed the matter by
telephone.  However, further analysis and discussions with other
city attorneys as well as a second discussion with Mr. Leidigh of
the FPPC must lead to the contrary conclusion as detailed herein.
This conclusion is reenforsed by a copy of a letter received on
February 17 from Mr. Leidigh which is attached hereto.

1.  Would a conflict of interest arise under Section 87100 based
upon the financial interests provisions of Section 87103 if
the councilmember votes on an exclusive franchise for the
Chula Vista Sanitary Service when the principal owner of said
franchisee is a client of the councilman who is a stockbroker

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Three


or account executive with whom said principal owner maintains
an account and the councilman handles the buying and selling
of his stocks and other investments receiving commissions in
excess of $250 from said account?

The opinion of the City Attorney that a conflict of interest
arises in the circumstance as stated in this question is predi-
cated on the dual basis of (a) the statutory restriction and (b)
the prohibition of sound public policy evolved from common law
principles. In examining any conflict of interest situation, the
Attorney General has followed such a practice traditionally as
noted in 40 OPS. CAL. ATTY. GEN. 210 (1962), 58 OPS. CAL. ATTY.
GEN. 345 (1975).

Section 87103 of the Government Code states:

"An official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more
in value provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the time when the
decision is made."

Other provisions of the section relating to a business entity
refer to a business entity controlled by a public official, which
means that the public official, his agents, spouse or dependent
children, held more than ten percent (10%) of the ownership
interest in the entity. This is obviously not the case of an
account executive for Merrill Lynch and, therefore, it is the
opinion of the City Attorney that only Section (c) is applicable.

In reviewing the case law, there seems little doubt that the
nature of that interest which distinguishes that held by the
public in general is an interest in business which is affected by
the discretionary judgment rendered by the public official. The
leading case on this matter is Witt v. Morrow 70 C.A.3d 813
(1977). In that case, the councilman, Mr. Morrow, was an employee

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Four


and president of Basic Economic Education, Inc. (BEE), a nonprofit
corporation making investments. Mr. Morrow received $550 a month.
BEE was the owner of three properties across the street from the
Linda Vista Shopping Center valued at more than $250,000. The
Redevelopment Agency of the City of San Diego, which is composed
of the members of the City Council, was involved in the redevelop-
ment of that center. As a result of the redevelopment process,
there would be an increase in the value of the property accruing
to BEE. The court held that Mr. Morrow's interest was not remote
simply because he only received a salary from BEE.

"Such an interpretation would mean as long as he was paid a
set salary by a third party, a public official would never
have a conflict of interest between his public duties and his
private employment because his interest would always be
remote.

Morrow argues there is no conflict of interest here because
there is no evidence his activities as president and attorney
of BEE and his receipt of $550 per month would be affected in
any way by any decision on this project. His concept of what
constitutes a conflict of interest is too narrow. It is not
just actual improprieties which the law seeks to forestall
but also the appearance of possible improprieties. Any
employer, in the private or public sector, wishes to keep his
job and maintain good relations with his employer. A person
who must make decisions which may affect his employer's purse
is in a situation where he may not give full consideration to
the merits of the decision." Witt v. Morrow 70 C.A.3d 817,
822-23. (Emphasis added)

The Morrow case is reflected in several Attorney General's
opinions such as the opinion found in 58 OPS. CAL. ATTY. GEN 345.
In that opinion, a question arose as to the possibility of a
conflict of interest with members of the Santa Monica Airport
Commission who made recommendations to the Santa Monica City
Council concerning charges to be levied for aircraft tie-down
spaces at the Santa Monica Municipal Airport where such members
are lessees of such tie-down spaces at such airport. In these
cases, as well as any others that can be found, it should be noted
that the conflict arises where the individual or a business entity
in which he has a clear interest would benefit as a result of the
decision being made in his capacity as a public official.

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Five


This is clearly not the case at hand. Councilman Scott would
receive commissions on any transactions that he would undertake on
behalf of the owner of the franchisee of the City as a broker
working for Merrill Lynch. The decision to grant the franchise to
said franchisee in no way increases or impacts that benefit or
financial gain either to the councilman or his employer. Even if
we were to view this situation in light of the "prohibition of
sound public policy evolved from common law principles", the same
conclusion must be reached.

The leading case of common law principles is People v. Darby 114
C.A.2d 412 (1952). In that case, the defendant was a member of
the Board of Education who owned a vacant store in the City of Los
Angeles which he desired to lease to the firm of Jack and Jill, to
engage in an ice cream business. He further as a member of the
Board of Education passed upon the bid of Jack and Jill to act as
as supplier of school ice cream.

"Appellant could help him get it if Jack and Jill were the
low bidder. Appellant thereupon injected into the discussion
the leasing of his vacant store and quoted a rental of $300
monthly and suggest his earnest desire to procure a good
tenant. People v. Darby 114 C.A.2d 412, 418

Again, though the Attorney General has stated:

"Fidelity in the public officer must be maintained, and the
law does not permit a public officer to place himself in a
position in which he might be tempted by his own private
interest to disregard the interests of the public." 40 OPS.
CAL. ATTY. GEN. 210, 212 (1962)

However, it is apparent that the financial interest spoken of in
Section 87103 is not limited to the benefit of the private
interest of the councilman or his employer. There is no con-
nection between the performance of Councilman Scott's official
duty and the benefit derived by his activities as a broker. The
stock transactions are in no way affected by the decision to grant
said franchise. Such is not the test. Although this office
originally viewed the requirements of Section 87103 as involving a
determination that the decisions in which the official partici-
pated must have an effect upon his interest greater than that of
the general public, it is evident that the section applies to the
material effect upon:

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Six


1. The effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease the annualized gross income of the engineer
developer by one percent or more, or the annual net
income of the engineer-developer by five tenths of
one percent (0.5%) or more;

2. The effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease the assets or liabilities of the engineer
developer by $50,000 or more, or by five tenths of
one percent (0.5%) of his current assets or liabilities,
whichever is less.

Commission Regulation 18702 also provides that:

In cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible to
apply, the financial effect of a governmental decision on a
financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if the decision
will have a substantial rather than insignificant effect on
the engineer-developer.

Therefore, it must be concluded that Councilman Scott should have
disqualified himself in the total decisionmaking process relative
to the grant of the franchise to Chula Vista Sanitary Service.
Having reached that conclusion, the question now arises as to what
is the appropriate remedial action. This question was posed to
Mr. Leidigh, Counsel for the Fair Political Practices Commission
and two courses of action were explored. It would be possible to
reconsider the entire issue of the grant of the franchise with Mr.
Scott declaring a possible conflict of interest and disqualifying
himself from the matter.

However, both Mr. Leidigh and myself agreed that since Mr. Scott's
vote in this decision was not determinative, the matter being a 5-0
decision, it would be possible to direct that his vote now be
recorded as an abstention, upon his statement for the record that
he so desires to abstain and to be removed completely from the
decision regarding the grant of the franchise to Chula Vista
Sanitary Service because of a conflict of interest. The City
Attorney has indicated to Mr. Scott that this would be his
recommendation. Mr. Scott has requested that this offer so
recommend that the City Council at its meeting of February 22,
1983 ask Mr. Scott to disqualify himself because of a conflict of
interest and request that the City Clerk now record the vote on
the matter of the grant of the franchise to the Chula Vista

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Seven


Sanitary Service as 4-0 with Councilman Scott abstaining because
of a conflict of interest.

The City Attorney has also informed Councilman Scott that he is
sending a copy of this opinion to Mr. Robert Leidigh requesting
confirmation of the advice contained herein. A copy has also been
forwarded to all members of the Board of Ethics, especially to Mr.
Roland who initially brought this matter to the attention of Mr.
Scott and the City Attorney. Mr. Roland is to be commended for
the manner in which he sought clarification of the issues
contained in this matter since they will clearly be beneficial to
the public interest by providing a basis for the conduct of city
councilmen in the future. Councilman Scott has indicated that he
will disseminate this opinion to his colleagues and members of the
press.

2.	What are the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section
87207 of the Government Code for a stockbroker or account
executive whose total income is now based solely upon
commissions received upon the sale or purchase of stocks
which he handles for his clients?

Councilman Scott, several years ago, posed the question as to
disclosure requirements in his position as a broker for Merrill
Lynch. He wished to know what was required in terms of completing
the disclosure statement required of all public officials pursuant
to Section 87207 of the Government Code of the State of California. At that time, this office stated that he need not disclose
the names of his clients based upon the fact that, first, he was
not the business entity as stated in said section nor did he have
any substantial interest in the firm of Merrill Lynch and,
secondly, that he received his primary income as a salary from the
firm of Merrill Lynch. At the present time, he now receives his
income from Merrill Lynch in the form of commissions generated by
sales or purchases of stocks on behalf of clients. It is evident
that Councilman Scott as an account executive in no way owns or
controls a 10% or more interest in the business entity as seems to
be the consistent reference throughout the Conflict of Interest
Code and, in fact, he does not own even a small 3% to 5% interest
giving him any remote interest in the company. However, adopting
a conservative view, the City Attorney advised Councilman Scott to
make disclosure in accordance with the views expressed herein and

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Eight


the matter will also be referred to the Fair Political Practices
Commission for confirmation of the advice rendered along with
Question No. 1.

If a person pays to a public official indirectly through payment
to a business entity in which the public official holds a 10%
interest, the level of income requiring disclosure depends on the
profession or business of the recipient. In the case of attorneys
or brokers, Section 87207 now states that disclosure must occur if
his pro rata share is $1,000 or more from a single source, whereas
if the recipient is employed in any other business or profession,
declaration and disclosure must occur if his pro rata share is
$10,000 or more.

     "We are at a loss to understand how the retainer fee--the
abuse of which simply constitutes one means among many for
effecting a bribe (to speak plainly) to a public
official--may be said to distinguish the attorney from all
others in a manner which bears a fair and reasonable
relationship to the relevant purpose of the Act. That
purpose, as we have indicated, is to insure that income of a
public official which may be 'materially affected' by his
official actions be disclosed. That purpose is not advanced
in a manner consistent with equal protection when one
profession is singled out for special treatment on the basis
of the availability to him of a method of payment which is no
more subject to abuse than any other which might be used to
further illicit ends.

     We have concluded for the foregoing reasons that the classifi-
cation here in question, insofar as it related to attorneys,
fails to exhibit any fair and reasonable relationship to
the stated legislative objective and therefore is in
violation of the principles of equal protection embodied in
our state and federal Constitutions. Although as we have
indicated no broker is a party to this action, we believe it
manifest from what we have said that the classification
insofar as it relates to brokers must fail as well. While we
agree that there may be good and adequate grounds for
distinguishing, for example, a real estate broker from a real
estate dealer (due to the profit margin differential
resulting from the inclusion of recovered investment in the
gross receipts of the latter), no grounds occur to us or have

Frank A. Scott
February 17, 1983
Page Nine


been suggested which would justify differentiating brokers
from all others with a similar profit margin--such as real
estate salesmen for example. In view of this we see no point
in dealing with the classification piecemeal. We therefore
hold that the classification before us, set forth in Section
87297, subdivision (b)(2), is invalid both as applied to
attorneys and as applied to brokers. In so doing we leave
the remainder of the section intact (see §81015), subdivision
(b)(3) to apply to all affected public officials regardless
of business profession. The disclosure as to personal
sources of income will continue as provided by subdivision
(a). Hays v. Wood 25 Cal.3d 772, 794-795 (1979)

Therefore, it is the opinion of the City Attorney that Councilman
Scott as a broker should make disclosure, but only in those
instances where his pro rata share of fees as a broker from any
single source during the reporting period was equal to or greater
than $10,000. As indicated, these matters will be forwarded to
the Fair Political Practices Commission for confirmation.

Telephone ... Enforcement ... Counsel of Interest
322-6441          322-6444

May 4, 1979


Anthony C. Benutti
Deputy City Attorney
151 West Marion Street
San Jose, CA 95110

Dear Mr. Benutti:

Thank you for your letter on behalf of Councilmember Jerry Betti requesting confirmation of advice rendered to your office regarding Mr. Estruth's obligations under the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act.

The facts as we understand them were that Mr. Estruth is employed as a broker for Dean Witter Reynolds. Mr. Estruth has received commissions in excess of $250 from this account. In November of 1978, the account was transferred to another broker in the office, and Mr. Estruth no longer receives commissions from the account.

Government Code Section 87100 provides that a public official cannot participate in governmental decisions in which he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that:

An official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made;...

Mr. Anthony C. Bernadotti                                    Page Two
May 4, 1979


Mr. Bernadotti has received $250 from the engineer-developer
within the last twelve months therefore, the engineer-developer
is a source of income to him for the purposes of Government
Code Section 3100 et seq. based on the facts provided
the engineer-developer will continue to be a source of income
to Mr. Bernadotti until November 1979. Because the engineer-developer is a source of income to Mr. Bernadotti, he must refrain
from participating or using his official position to influence any governmental decision that may materially affect
the engineer-developer in a manner distinguishable from the
public generally.

Given that engineer-developer functions as a business
entity, the following standards should be applied in determining whether the engineer-developer will be materially
affected by a governmental decision:

(1) The effect of the decision will be to increase
or decrease the annualized gross income of
the engineer-developer by one percent or
more, or the annual net income of the engineer-developer by five tenths of one percent
(0.5%) or more;

(2) The effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease the assets or liabilities
of the engineer-developer by $50,000 or
more, or by five tenths of one percent
(0.5%) of his current assets or liabilities,
whichever is less.

See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702

Commission Regulation 18726 also provides that: In
cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible to apply,
the financial officer or a governmental decision on a financial interest of Mr. Bernadotti is material if the decision
will have a substantial rather than insignificant effect on
the engineer-developer.

I hope that our advice is helpful. If you have any
questions regarding this matter, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Dwight P. Dickerson
Legal Counsel
Conflicts of Interest Division

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY
CITY OF SAN JOSE

ROBERT J. LOGAN
CITY ATTORNEY

151 WEST MISSION STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95110
(408) 277-4434

April 12, 1970

California Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 207
Sacramento, CA 95864

Attn: Dwight Dickerson, Esq.

Re: Councilman Jerry Estruth

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

Mr. Estruth has asked me to confirm the conclusions reached in a
series of phone calls among yourself, Diane Ice and myself concerning
Mr. Estruth's obligations under the Political Reform Act relating to
a source of income.

As you will recall, Mr. Estruth works for the brokerage firm of
Dean Witter Reynolds in Palo Alto. He was elected to the San Jose
City Council in November of 1969, and took office on January 1, 1970.
Up until some time in November of 1978, Mr. Estruth handled the stock
account of an engineer-developer who is quite active in real estate
development in San Jose, both on his own behalf and on behalf of others.
In November, Mr. Estruth turned the stock account over to another broker
in the firm and received his last share of commissions on said account
in an amount exceeding $250.00.

With respect to the disqualification provisions of the Act, our
conclusions may be summarized as follows:

1. Mr. Estruth has a financial interest in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the engineer-
developer from whom he received $250.00 or more within
twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made.

The engineer-developer is a source of income for Mr. Estruth.

Mr. Estruth must refrain from making, participating in the
making, or in any way attempting to use his official posi-
tion to influence any governmental decisions which he knows
or has reason to know would have any of the following effects
on the engineer-developer, or on Mr. Estruth:

California Fair Political Practices
Commission
Page 2
April 12, 1979

A. The engineer-developer would be affected in any of the
following ways:

(1) The effect of the decision will be to increase
or decrease the annualized gross income of the
engineer-developer by one percent or more, or
the annual net income of the engineer-developer
by five months of one percent (0.5%) or more;

(2) The effect of the decision will be to increase
or decrease the assets or liabilities of the
engineer-developer by $50,000.00 or more, or
by five months of one percent (0.5%) of his
current assets or liabilities, whichever is less.

B. In cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible
to apply, the financial effect of a governmental decision
on a financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if
the decision will have a substantial rather than insigni-
ficant effect on the engineer-developer.

Again, thank you for your assistance in this matter, and if this does
not reflect your understanding of our discussions, please contact the
undersigned.

Very truly yours,

ROBERT J. LOGAN
City Attorney

by [signature]
ANTHONY C. BENNETTI
Deputy City Attorney

violation of the principles of equal protection embodied in 
our state and federal Constitutions. 
Although as we have 
indicated no broker is a party to this action, we believe it 
manifest from what we have said that the classification 
insofar as it relates to brokers must fail as well. 
While we 
agree that there may be good and adequate grounds for 
distinguishing, for example, a real estate broker from a real 
estate dealer (due to the profit margin differential 
resulting from the inclusion of recovered investment in the 
gross receipts of the latter), no grounds occur to us or have 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Nine 
been suggested which would justify differentiating brokers 
from all others with a similar profit margin--such as real 
estate salesmen for example. 
In view of this we see no point 
in dealing with the classification piecemeal. 
We therefore 
hold that the classification before us, set forth in Section 
87297, subdivision (b)(2), is invalid both as applied to 
attorneys and as applied to brokers. 
In so doing, we leave 
the remainder of the section intact (see §81015), subdivision 
(b){3) to apply to all affected public officials regardless 
of business profession. 
The disclosure as to personal 
sources of income will continue as provided by subdivision 
(a). 
Hays v. Wood 25 Cal.3d 772, 794-795 (1979) 
Therefore, it is the opinion of the City Attorney that Councilman 
Scott as a broker should make disclosure, but only in those 
instances where his pro rata share of fees as a broker from any 
single source during the reporting period was equal to or greater 
than $10,000. 
As indicated, these matters will be forwarded to 
the Fair Political Practices Commission for confirmation.

The leading case of common law principles is People v. Darby 114 
C.A.2d 412 (1952). 
In that case, the defendant was a member of 
the Board of Education who owned a vacant store in the City of Los 
Angeles which he desired to lease to the firm of Jack and Jill, to 
engage in an ice cream business. 
He further as a member of the 
Board of Education passed upon the bid of Jack and Jill to act as 
as supplier of school ice cream. 
"Appellant could help him get it if Jack and Jill were the 
low bidder. 
Appellant thereupon injected into the discussion 
the leasing of his vacant store and quoted a rental of $300 
monthly and suggest his earnest desire to procure a good 
tenant. 
People v. Darby 114 C.A.2d 412, 418 
Again, although the Attorney General has stated: 
"Fidelity in the public officer must be maintained, and the 
law does not permit a public officer to place himself in a 
position in which he might be tempted by his own private 
interest to disregard the interests of the public." 
40 OPS. 
CAL. ATTY. GEN. 210, 212 (1962) 
However, it is apparent that the financial interest spoken of in 
Section 87103 is not limited to the benefit of the private 
interest of the councilman or his employer. 
There is no con-
nection between the performance of Councilman Scott's official 
duty and the benefit derived by his activities as a broker. 
The 
stock transactions are in no way affected by the decision to grant 
said franchise. 
Such is not the test. 
Although this office 
originally viewed the requirements of Section 87103 as involving a 
determination that the decisions in which the official partici-
pated must have an effect upon his interest greater than that of 
the general public, it is evident that the section applies to the 
material ef 
ct upon: 


Frank A. Scott

Page Six 
1. 
The effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease the annualized gross income of the engineer 
developer by one percent or more, or the annual net 
income of the engineer-developer by five tenths of 
one percent (0.5%) or more; 
2. 
The effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease the assets or liabilities of the engineer 
developer by $50,000 or more, or by five tenths of 
one percent (0.5%) of his current assets or liabilities, 
whichever is less. 
Commission Regulation 18702 also provides that: 
In cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible to 
apply, the financial effect of a governmental decision on a 
financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if the decision 
will have a substantial rather than insignificant effect on 
the engineer-developer. 
Therefore, it must be concluded that Councilman Scott should have 
disqualified himself in the total decisionmaking process relative 
to the grant of the franchise to Chula Vista Sanitary Service. 
Having reached that conclusion, the question now arises as to what 
is the appropriate remedial action. 
This question was posed to 
Hr. Leidigh, Counsel for the Fair Political Practices Commission 
and two courses of action were explored. 
It would be possible to 
reconsider the entire issue of the grant of the franchise with Mr. 
Scott declaring a possible conflict of interest and disqualifiying 
himself from the matter. 
However, both Mr. Leidigh and myself agreed that since Mr. Scott's 
vote in this decision was not determinative, the matter being a 
5-0 decision, it would be possible to direct that his vote now be 
recorded as an abstention, upon his statement for the record that 
he so desires to abstain and to be removed completely from the 
decision regarding the grant of the franchise to Chula Vista 
Sanitary Service because of a conflict of interest. 
The City 
Attorney has indicated to Mr. Scott that this would be his 
recommendation. 
Mr. Scott has requested that this office so 
recommend that the City Council at its meeting of February 22, 
1983 ask Mr. Scott to disqualify himself because of a conflict of 
interest and request that the City Clerk now record the vote on 
the matter of the grant of the franchise to the Chula Vista 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Seven 
Sanitary Service as 4-0 with Councilman Scott abstaining because 
of a conflict of interest. 
The City Attorney has also informed Councilman Scott that he is 
sending a copy of this opinion to Mr. Robert Leidigh requesting 
confirmation of the advice contained herein. 
A copy has also been 
forwarded to all members of the Board of Ethics, especially to Mr. 
Roland who initially brought this matter to the attention of Mr. 
Scott and the City Attorney. 
Mr. Roland is to be commended for 
the manner in which he sought clarification of the issues 
contained in this matter since they will clearly be beneficial to 
the public interest by providing a basis for the conduct of city 
councilmen in the future. 
Councilman Scott has indicated that he 
will disseminate this opinion to his colleagues and members of the 
press. 
2. 
What are the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section 
87207 of the Government Code for a stockbroker or account 
executive whose total income is now based solely upon 
commissions received upon the sale or purchase of stocks 
which he handles for his clients? 
Councilman Scott, several years ago, posed the question as to 
disclosure requirements in his position as a broker for Merrill 
Lynch. 
He wished to know what was required in terms of completing 
the disclosure statement required of all public officials pursuant 
to Section 87207 of the Government Code of the State of Califor-
nia. 
At that time, this office stated that he need not disclose 
the names of his clients based upon the fact that, first, he was 
not the business entity as stated in said section nor did he have 
any substantial interest in the firm of Merrill Lynch and, 
secondly, that he received his primary income as a salary from the 
firm of Merrill Lynch. 
At the present time, he now receives his 
income from Merrill Lynch in the form of commissions generated by 
sales or purchases of stocks on behalf of clients. 
It is evident 
that Councilman Scott as an account executive in no way owns or 
controls a 10% or more interest in the business entity as seems to 
be the consistent reference throughout the Conflict of Interest 
Code and, in fact, he does not own even a small 3% to 5% interest 
giving him any remote interest in the company. 
However, adopting 
a conservative view, the City Attorney advised Councilman Scott to 
make disclosure in accordance with the views expressed herein and

the matter will also be referred to the Fair Political Practices

sections.analysis:
On Friday, February 11, 1983, the City Attorney was requested by 
Councilman Frank Scott to provide a written opinion concerning the 
above questions principally the first of which was raised by Mr. 
Clifford Roland, a member of the Board of Ethics of the City of 
Chula Vista. 
In addition to a review of the conflicts of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act, certain opinions of the 
Attorney General and Appellate Court cases cited herein, the City 
Attorney contacted other city attorneys in this area who have 
presented papers to the League of California Cities concerning the 
matter of conflict of interest. 
As a result of this analysis and 
basing the opinion primarily upon the fact that the decision 
regarding the award of the franchise to Chula Vista Sanitary 
Sanitary Service did not directly or materially affect the 
financial interests of the councilman acting as a broker, the City 
Attorney in a meeting with Mr. Roland and Councilman Scott 
expressed the opinion that there was no conflict of interest. 
He further indicated at that time that this viewpoint was shared 
by those persons with whom he had discussed the matter and that he 
had received no clear reading of the situation from Mr. Robert E. 
Leidigh, Counsel for the State of California Fair Political 
Practices Commission with whom he also discussed the matter by 
telephone. 
However, further analysis and discussions with other 
city attorneys as well as a second discussion with Mr. Leidigh of 
the FPPC must lead to the contrary conclusion as detailed herein. 
This conclusion is reenforced by a copy of a letter received on 
February 17 from Mr. Leidigh which is attached hereto. 
1. 
Would a conflict of interest arise under Section 87100 based 
upon the financial interests provisions of Section 87103 if 
the councilrnernber votes on an exclusive franchise for the 
Chula Vista Sanitary Service when the principal owner of said 
franchisee is a client of the councilman who is a stockbroker 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Three 
or account executive with whom said principal owner maintains 
an account and the councilman handles the buying and selling 
of his stocks and other investments receiving commissions in 
excess of $250 from said account? 
The opinion of the City Attorney that a conflict of interest 
arises in the circumstance as stated in this question is predi-
cated on the dual basis of (a) the statutory restriction; and (b) 
the prohibition of sound public policy evolved from common law 
principles. 
In examining any conflict of interest situation, the 
Attorney General has followed such a practice traditionally as 
noted in 40 OPS. CAL. ATTY. GEN. 210 (1962), 58 OPS. CAL. ATTY. 
GEN. 345 (1975). 
Section 87103 of the Government Code states: 
"An official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: 
(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial 
lending institution in the regular course of business on 
terms available to the public without regard to official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more 
in value provided to, received by or promised to the public 
official within twelve months prior to the time when the 
decision is made." 
Other provisions of the section relating to a business entity 
refer to a business entity controlled by a public official, which 
means that the public official, his agents, spouse or dependent 
children, held more than ten percent (10%) of the ownership 
interest in the entity. 
This is obviously not the case of an 
account executive for Merrill Lynch and, therefore, it is the 
opinion of the City Attorney that only Section {c} is applicable. 
In reviewing the case law, there seems little doubt that the 
nature of that interest which distinguishes that held by the 
public in general is an interest in business which is affected by 
the discretionary judgment rendered by the public official. 
The 
leading case on this matter is Witt v. Morrow 70 C.A.3d 813 
(1977). 
In that case, the councilman, Mr. Morrow, was an employee 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Four 
and president of Basic Economic Education, Inc. (BEE), a nonprofit 
corporation making investments. 
Mr. Morrow received $550 a month. 
BEE was the owner of three properties across the street from the 
Linda vista Shopping Center valued at more than $250,000. 
The 
Redevelopment Agency of the City of San Diego, which is composed 
of the members of the City Council, was involved in the redevelop-
ment of that center. 
As a result of the redevelopment process, 
there would be an increase in the value of the property accruing 
to BEE. 
The court held that Mr. Morrow's interest was not remote 
simply because he only received a salary from BEE. 
"Such an interpretation would mean as long as he was paid a 
set salary by a third party, a public official would never 
have a conflict of interest between his public duties and his 
private employment because his interest would always be 
remote. 
Morrow argues there is no conflict of interest here because 
there is no evidence his activities as president and attorney 
of BEE and his receipt of $550 per month would be affected in 
any way by any decision on this project. 
His concept of what 
constitutes a conflict of interest is too narrow. 
It is not 
just actual improprieties which the law seeks to forestall 
but also the appearance of possible improprieties. 
Any 
employer, in the private or public sector, wishes to keep his 
job and maintain good relations with his employer. 
A person 
who must make decisions which may affect his employer's purse 
is in a situation where he may not give full consideration to 
the merits of the decision." 
Witt v. Morrow 70 C.A.3d 817, 
822-23. 
(Emphasis added) 
The Morrow case is reflected in several Attorney General's 
opinions such as the opinion found in 58 OPS. CAL. ATTY. GEN 345. 
In that opinion, a question arose as to the possibility of a 
conflict of interest with members of the Santa Monica Airport 
Commission who made recommendations to the Santa Monica City 
Council concerning charges to be levied for aircraft tie-down 
spaces at the Santa Monica Municipal Airport where such members 
are lessees of such tie-down spaces at such airport. 
In these 
cases, as well as any others that can be found, it should be noted 
that the conflict arises where the individual or a business entity 
in which he has a clear interest would benefit as a result of the 
decision being made in his capacity as a public official. 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Five 
This is clearly not the case at hand. 
Councilman Scott would 
receive commissions on any transactions that he would undertake on 
behalf of the owner of the franchisee of the City as a broker 
working for Merrill Lynch. 
The decision to grant the franchise to 
said franchisee in no way increases or impacts that benefit or 
financial gain either to the councilman or his employer. 
Even if 
we were to view this situation in light of the "prohibition of 
sound public policy evolved from common law principles", the same

QUESTION: No.1. 
If a person pays to a public official indirectly through payment 
to a business entity in which the public official holds a 10% 
interest, the level of income requiring disclosure depends on the 
profession or business of the recipient. 
In the case of attorneys 
or brokers, Section 87207 now states that disclosure must occur if 
his pro rata share is $1,000 or more from a single source, whereas 
if the recipient is employed in any other business or profession, 
declaration and disclosure must occur if his pro rata share is 
$10,000 or more. 
"We are at a loss to understand how the retainer fee--the 
abuse of which simply constitutes one means among many for 
effecting a bribe (to speak plainly) to a public 
official--may be said to distinguish the attorney from all 
others in a manner which bears a fair and reasonable 
relationship to the relevant purpose of the Act. 
That 
purpose, as we have indicated, is to insure that income of a 
public official which may be 'materially affected' by his 
official actions be disclosed. 
That purpose is not advanced 
in a manner consistent with equal protection when one 
profession is singled out for special treatment on the basis 
of the availability to him of a method of payment which is no 
more subject to abuse than any other which might be used to 
further illicit ends. 
We have concluded for the foregoing reasons that the classifi-
cation here in question, insofar as it related to attorneys, 
fails to exhibit any fair and reasonable relationship to 
the stated legislative objective and therefore is in 
violation of the principles of equal protection embodied in 
our state and federal Constitutions. 
Although as we have 
indicated no broker is a party to this action, we believe it 
manifest from what we have said that the classification 
insofar as it relates to brokers must fail as well. 
While we 
agree that there may be good and adequate grounds for 
distinguishing, for example, a real estate broker from a real 
estate dealer (due to the profit margin differential 
resulting from the inclusion of recovered investment in the 
gross receipts of the latter), no grounds occur to us or have 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Nine 
been suggested which would justify differentiating brokers 
from all others with a similar profit margin--such as real 
estate salesmen for example. 
In view of this we see no point 
in dealing with the classification piecemeal. 
We therefore 
hold that the classification before us, set forth in Section 
87297, subdivision (b)(2), is invalid both as applied to 
attorneys and as applied to brokers. 
In so doing, we leave 
the remainder of the section intact (see §81015), subdivision 
(b){3) to apply to all affected public officials regardless 
of business profession. 
The disclosure as to personal 
sources of income will continue as provided by subdivision 
(a). 
Hays v. Wood 25 Cal.3d 772, 794-795 (1979) 
Therefore, it is the opinion of the City Attorney that Councilman 
Scott as a broker should make disclosure, but only in those 
instances where his pro rata share of fees as a broker from any 
single source during the reporting period was equal to or greater 
than $10,000. 
As indicated, these matters will be forwarded to 
the Fair Political Practices Commission for confirmation.

CONCLUSION: must be reached. 
The leading case of common law principles is People v. Darby 114 
C.A.2d 412 (1952). 
In that case, the defendant was a member of 
the Board of Education who owned a vacant store in the City of Los 
Angeles which he desired to lease to the firm of Jack and Jill, to 
engage in an ice cream business. 
He further as a member of the 
Board of Education passed upon the bid of Jack and Jill to act as 
as supplier of school ice cream. 
"Appellant could help him get it if Jack and Jill were the 
low bidder. 
Appellant thereupon injected into the discussion 
the leasing of his vacant store and quoted a rental of $300 
monthly and suggest his earnest desire to procure a good 
tenant. 
People v. Darby 114 C.A.2d 412, 418 
Again, although the Attorney General has stated: 
"Fidelity in the public officer must be maintained, and the 
law does not permit a public officer to place himself in a 
position in which he might be tempted by his own private 
interest to disregard the interests of the public." 
40 OPS. 
CAL. ATTY. GEN. 210, 212 (1962) 
However, it is apparent that the financial interest spoken of in 
Section 87103 is not limited to the benefit of the private 
interest of the councilman or his employer. 
There is no con-
nection between the performance of Councilman Scott's official 
duty and the benefit derived by his activities as a broker. 
The 
stock transactions are in no way affected by the decision to grant 
said franchise. 
Such is not the test. 
Although this office 
originally viewed the requirements of Section 87103 as involving a 
determination that the decisions in which the official partici-
pated must have an effect upon his interest greater than that of 
the general public, it is evident that the section applies to the 
material ef 
ct upon: 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Six 
1. 
The effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease the annualized gross income of the engineer 
developer by one percent or more, or the annual net 
income of the engineer-developer by five tenths of 
one percent (0.5%) or more; 
2. 
The effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease the assets or liabilities of the engineer 
developer by $50,000 or more, or by five tenths of 
one percent (0.5%) of his current assets or liabilities, 
whichever is less. 
Commission Regulation 18702 also provides that: 
In cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible to 
apply, the financial effect of a governmental decision on a 
financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if the decision 
will have a substantial rather than insignificant effect on 
the engineer-developer. 
Therefore, it must be concluded that Councilman Scott should have 
disqualified himself in the total decisionmaking process relative 
to the grant of the franchise to Chula Vista Sanitary Service. 
Having reached that conclusion, the question now arises as to what 
is the appropriate remedial action. 
This question was posed to 
Hr. Leidigh, Counsel for the Fair Political Practices Commission 
and two courses of action were explored. 
It would be possible to 
reconsider the entire issue of the grant of the franchise with Mr. 
Scott declaring a possible conflict of interest and disqualifiying 
himself from the matter. 
However, both Mr. Leidigh and myself agreed that since Mr. Scott's 
vote in this decision was not determinative, the matter being a 
5-0 decision, it would be possible to direct that his vote now be 
recorded as an abstention, upon his statement for the record that 
he so desires to abstain and to be removed completely from the 
decision regarding the grant of the franchise to Chula Vista 
Sanitary Service because of a conflict of interest. 
The City 
Attorney has indicated to Mr. Scott that this would be his 
recommendation. 
Mr. Scott has requested that this office so 
recommend that the City Council at its meeting of February 22, 
1983 ask Mr. Scott to disqualify himself because of a conflict of 
interest and request that the City Clerk now record the vote on 
the matter of the grant of the franchise to the Chula Vista 


Frank A. Scott 
February 17, 1983 
Page Seven 
Sanitary Service as 4-0 with Councilman Scott abstaining because 
of a conflict of interest. 
The City Attorney has also informed Councilman Scott that he is 
sending a copy of this opinion to Mr. Robert Leidigh requesting 
confirmation of the advice contained herein. 
A copy has also been 
forwarded to all members of the Board of Ethics, especially to Mr. 
Roland who initially brought this matter to the attention of Mr. 
Scott and the City Attorney. 
Mr. Roland is to be commended for 
the manner in which he sought clarification of the issues 
contained in this matter since they will clearly be beneficial to 
the public interest by providing a basis for the conduct of city 
councilmen in the future. 
Councilman Scott has indicated that he 
will disseminate this opinion to his colleagues and members of the 
press. 
2. 
What are the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section 
87207 of the Government Code for a stockbroker or account 
executive whose total income is now based solely upon 
commissions received upon the sale or purchase of stocks 
which he handles for his clients? 
Councilman Scott, several years ago, posed the question as to 
disclosure requirements in his position as a broker for Merrill 
Lynch. 
He wished to know what was required in terms of completing 
the disclosure statement required of all public officials pursuant 
to Section 87207 of the Government Code of the State of Califor-
nia. 
At that time, this office stated that he need not disclose 
the names of his clients based upon the fact that, first, he was 
not the business entity as stated in said section nor did he have 
any substantial interest in the firm of Merrill Lynch and, 
secondly, that he received his primary income as a salary from the 
firm of Merrill Lynch. 
At the present time, he now receives his 
income from Merrill Lynch in the form of commissions generated by 
sales or purchases of stocks on behalf of clients. 
It is evident 
that Councilman Scott as an account executive in no way owns or

I have had two telephone conversations with you in the last month. The first occurred on February 9th. You called to inquire about the applicability of our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18740 to two councilmembers' circumstances. This regulation permits, in certain limited circumstances, the non-disclosure of privileged information otherwise required to be reported on an official's Statement of Economic Interests. We specifically discussed its applicability to two situations: (1) someone who loans money to individuals, but who is not a bank; and (2) someone who is a stockbroker. I advised you that the application of Section 18740 is limited to legally recognized privileges and, even then, would not apply in all cases. I cited you to the case of Hays v. Wood, in which an attorney asserted the attorney-client privilege, this privilege was rejected as a basis for non-disclosure by the California Supreme Court. Our first conversation did not involve any discussion of disqualification. The second phone conversation occurred on February 15th. In that conversation you asked me whether a councilmember who is a broker working for Merrill Lynch has to disclose names of clients who have paid him commissions. We discussed the Commission's past advice in this regard and I advised you that George Lindberg March 9, 1983 Page 2 the broker would have to disclose at the $10,000 and above level, but disqualification would be required at the $250 and above level. I mentioned the Bennetti letter, (A-2123) and you requested a copy of it, which I sent to you. You stated your opinion that the councilmember's disqualification is not required if the amount of his stockbroker commissions is not affected by the decision. I explained to you that it is the effect on the source of income which is determinative and cited you to the case of Witt v. Morrow in addition to the plain language of Government Code Section 87103. You then advised me that you would probably be seeking the Commission's advice as well as the Attorney General's because a councilmember may have acted on a matter when he had a conflict of interest.
